title: Object and environment tracking via shared sensor
abstract: One disclosed example provides a head-mounted device configured to control a plurality of light sources of a handheld object and acquire image data comprising a sequence of environmental tracking exposures in which the plurality of light sources are controlled to have a lower integrated intensity and handheld object tracking exposures in which the plurality of light sources are controlled to have a higher integrated intensity. The instructions are further executable to detect, via an environmental tracking exposure, one or more features of the surrounding environment, determine a pose of the head-mounted device based upon the one or more features of the surrounding environment detected, detect via a handheld object tracking exposure the plurality of light sources of the handheld object, determine a pose of the handheld object relative to the head-mounted device based upon the plurality of light sources detected, and output the pose of the handheld object.
claims:
Claims 1. A head-mounted device, comprising: an image sensing system; a logic device configured to execute instructions; and a storage device comprising instructions executable by the logic device to control a plurality of light sources of a handheld object; acquire image data of a surrounding environment, the image data comprising a sequence of environmental tracking exposures in which the plurality of light sources are controlled to have a lower integrated intensity and handheld object tracking exposures in which the plurality of light sources are controlled to have a higher integrated intensity; detect, via an environmental tracking exposure, one or more features of the surrounding environment; determine a pose of the head-mounted device based upon the one or more features of the surrounding environment detected; detect, via a handheld object tracking exposure, the plurality of light sources of the handheld object; determine a pose of the handheld object relative to the head-mounted device based upon the plurality of light sources detected; and output the pose of the handheld object determined. 2. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein the image sensing system comprises a stereo camera arrangement. 3. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein the image sensing system comprises one or more visible light camera sensors. 4. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein the instructions are executable to modulate an intensity of the plurality of light sources at a frequency of 90 Hz or greater. 5. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein the instructions are executable to acquire the handheld object tracking exposures at a higher frame rate than the environmental tracking exposures. 6. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein acquiring the environmental tracking exposures comprises one or more of a longer exposure time and a higher gain than acquiring the handheld object tracking exposures. 7. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein the sequence of environmental tracking exposures and handheld object tracking exposures comprises a ratio of one environmental tracking exposure to two handheld object tracking exposures. 8. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein acquisition of the environmental tracking exposure overlaps with an illumination of the plurality of light sources of the handheld object. 9. The head-mounted device of claim 1, wherein the instructions are executable to output one or more light pulse clusters, each light pulse cluster comprising an exposure light pulse and an antiflicker light pulse. 10. On a head-mounted device comprising an image sensing system, a logic device configured to execute instructions, and a storage device comprising instructions executable by the logic device, a method comprising: controlling a plurality of light sources of a handheld object; acquiring, via the image sensing system, image data of a surrounding environment, the image data comprising a sequence of environmental tracking exposures and handheld object tracking exposures; determining whether the handheld object is detectable in one or more handheld object tracking exposures; and based upon the handheld object not being detectable in the one or more handheld object tracking exposures, performing an optical time synchronization between a clock domain of the head-mounted device and a clock domain of the handheld object. 11. The method of claim 10, wherein determining whether the handheld object is detectable in the one or more handheld object tracking exposures comprises determining whether a plurality of visible light sources of the handheld object is detectable in the one or more handheld object tracking exposures. 12. The method of claim 10, wherein performing the optical time synchronization comprises performing the optical time synchronization via a binary search process comprising pulsing a plurality of light sources of the handheld object on for a first portion of a time interval and off for a second portion of the time interval, acquiring, via the image sensing system, a first handheld object tracking exposure, determining whether the first handheld tracking exposure occurred during the first portion or the second portion of the time interval, based on determining that the first handheld tracking exposure occurred during the first portion of the time interval, reducing a light pulse width to a first subinterval of the first portion of the time interval, acquiring, via the image sensing system, a second handheld object tracking 

 exposure, determining whether the second handheld object tracking exposure occurred during the first subinterval, and based on determining that the second handheld object tracking exposure occurred during the first subinterval, reducing the light pulse width to a second subinterval of the first subinterval. 13. The method of claim 10, wherein performing the optical time synchronization comprises acquiring a plurality of handheld object tracking exposures in which an illumination of the plurality of light sources in each exposure is adjusted by an uncertainty time relative to an exposure time; and determining, based upon a brightness of each handheld object tracking exposure acquired, a time offset between the clock domain of the handheld object and the clock domain of the head-mounted device. 14. The method of claim 10, wherein performing the optical time synchronization comprises, upon the handheld object being determined to be in motion or predicted to be in motion, controlling one or more light source of the handheld object to pulse in a pattern; acquiring, via the image sensing system, a first handheld object tracking exposure; and comparing the pattern expected to a pattern detected in the first handheld object tracking exposure. 15. The method of claim 10, wherein performing the optical time synchronization comprises based upon the handheld object being undetectable for a threshold number of handheld object tracking exposures, adjusting one or more of an intensity of a light pulse and a timing between light pulses to overlap a first light pulse with a first handheld object tracking exposure and overlap a second light pulse with a second handheld object tracking exposure, comparing an intensity of a first light pulse detected in a first handheld object tracking exposure to an intensity of a second light pulse detected in a second handheld object tracking exposure, and based upon the comparison, adjusting a relative timing of the clock domain of the handheld object and the clock domain of the head-mounted device.
